ontology.foodex2_description: Tom yum soup (Thai spicy and sour soup)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated tom yam soup code; A0CKW (composite dish — soup) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for tom yam soup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tom yam soup as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 350 g including broth, seafood, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Soup (Prawn) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central composite soup entries
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA table provides the primary nutrient profile for tom yum prawn soup. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for shrimp-based soups (SR Legacy) to validate macro ranges. Per-100g values represent the combined broth-and-solids composition as consumed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood broth soup with low carbohydrate density
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tom yam soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood broth soups reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100g) and high water content support a low GI classification. GL per serving calculated as (35 × 11.2 g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 3.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Tom yam soup contains approximately 480 mg sodium per 100g (1680 mg per standard 350g bowl), which represents a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or consume smaller portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom yam soup has a low glycemic index (estimated 35) and low carbohydrate density (3.2 g/100g), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 3.9 per bowl, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing blood glucose levels when consumed without added rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tom yam soup provides lean seafood protein and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, its high sodium content (approximately 1680 mg per bowl) poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose versions with reduced fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yam Soup (ต้มยำ) is a classic Thai hot and sour soup originating in Thailand and widely popular across Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it is commonly served in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls. The soup is characterised by its intensely aromatic, spicy, and tangy broth made by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai bird's eye chillies, fish sauce, and lime juice. It is most commonly prepared with prawns (Tom Yam Goong) but versions with mixed seafood, chicken, or mushrooms are also prevalent. The broth is typically clear or slightly cloudy, with a bold umami depth from fish sauce balanced by the brightness of lime and the heat of fresh chillies. Nutritionally, tom yam soup is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate dish (approximately 38 kcal and 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100g) with a moderate protein contribution from seafood (3.8 g/100g). Its primary clinical concern is high sodium content due to fish sauce, which can reach 1680 mg per standard bowl, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (estimated 35) and low glycemic load make it suitable for blood glucose management. The soup is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is generally considered halal when prepared with permissible seafood. It is rich in aromatic phytochemicals from lemongrass and galangal that may have anti-inflammatory properties. In Singapore, tom yam soup is frequently served as a standalone dish or as a base for noodle soups.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian food composition data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy shrimp soup entries. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for tom yam soup was identified. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved primary source provides an exact match for this specific dish as served in Singapore.
notes: 1. The input record tagged this dish as Filipino cuisine, which is incorrect; tom yam soup is a Thai dish originating in Thailand and is classified accordingly. The cuisine tag has been corrected to Thai. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in naturally prepared seafood soups with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA category data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (35, low) based on Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for comparable low-carbohydrate seafood broth soups; no direct GI measurement for tom yam soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Multilingual names include Thai script (ต้มยำ) as mandatory for Thai cuisine, plus Singaporean mandatory languages (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) reflecting the dish's widespread presence in Singapore's multicultural food landscape. 8. The input cuisine tags (Filipino, Noodle, Coffee, Cake) are inconsistent with this dish and have been disregarded; the correct classification is Thai soup.

Added Singaporean mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) reflecting Singapore serving context.
Sourced nutrient values from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy instead of relying on field survey input.
Estimated GI from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables with full documentation.
